evidence: All 10+ HTTP handlers use identical error handling via HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling, mapping IllegalArgumentException→400, RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, and generic Exception→500
Shared param helpers (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) are used uniformly across all handlers — no raw Integer.parseInt at call sites
ExplorationService defines typed options objects (SingleSeedExplorationOptions, ExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) keeping all three exploration methods to 3 parameters

buildSharedExploreResponse unifies the JSON envelope structure across explore, explore-multi, and compare endpoints with ExploreParameterExtras/ExploreVariantFields distinguishing what goes in 'parameters' vs top level

populateComparisonResponse emits 'adjectives' and 'adjectives_count' keys while populateExploreResponse emits 'seed_collocates'/'discovered_nouns'/'core_collocates' — semantically different layouts for the same conceptual operation (finding shared collocates) on related endpoints
ExplorationService.fetchExamples signature uses 'headword' for the second parameter, but every HTTP handler and HTTP parameter name uses 'seed' — the interface-to-HTTP vocabulary is inconsistent

handleSemanticFieldExamples response includes 'total_results' field; handleConcordanceExamples response includes 'fallback' field — these are structurally parallel endpoints (both return concordance examples for seed+collocate) but their response shapes diverge

QueryExecutor exposes four overlapping query-execution methods: executeCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern — distinctions are documented but the surface requires callers to know which variant to use for each scenario

strengths: Uniform error contract: every HTTP endpoint is wrapped by wrapWithErrorHandling, producing consistent 400/413/500 responses without per-handler boilerplate
Shared param-parsing utilities (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) are used consistently across all handlers — no raw parse calls scattered through handler code
ExplorationService methods use typed options objects instead of long flat parameter lists, keeping method arity at 3 across all four service methods

buildSharedExploreResponse + ExploreParameterExtras/ExploreVariantFields provide an explicit, reusable envelope pattern that correctly unifies explore, explore-multi, and compare response shapes at the outer layer

Handler separation (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) is consistent with route registration, giving each handler a coherent and limited responsibility
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The issues are localized naming and shape inconsistencies at the seam between the service layer and the HTTP response layer — not architectural chaos, but visible enough that a client integrating all three exploration endpoints would encounter surprising differences.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API surface has strong mechanical consistency: error handling, param parsing, and options objects all follow clear patterns that a developer would quickly learn. What pulls the score down is a cluster of shape and naming divergences at the response-assembly level: the compare endpoint serializes 'adjectives' while explore uses 'seed_collocates', fetchExamples uses 'headword' while HTTP uses 'seed', and the two example-fetching endpoints emit slightly different response fields despite being conceptually parallel. These are real friction points for clients but do not reflect deep structural inconsistency. Against the global anchors this sits between 'solid but uneven' (80) and 'significant drag' (60), closer to the upper end because the foundation is coherent and the issues are confined to response-body naming rather than protocol or method-ordering problems.

issues.summary: compare endpoint response uses 'adjectives' key while explore endpoints use 'seed_collocates', making the API surface inconsistent for a grammatical-relation-agnostic system
evidence: populateComparisonResponse puts 'adjectives' and 'adjectives_count' into the response map (ExploreResponseAssembler.java)
populateExploreResponse puts 'seed_collocates', 'discovered_nouns', 'core_collocates' — no 'adjectives' key anywhere in explore responses
The system is explicitly grammatical-relation-agnostic (compare works across all relations), yet 'adjectives' as a key name hardcodes the adj_predicate use case into the public API response shape
issues.suggestion: Rename the 'adjectives' / 'adjectives_count' keys in populateComparisonResponse to 'collocates' / 'collocates_count' to match the neutral vocabulary used throughout the rest of the exploration API. Update any frontend consumers accordingly.

issues.summary: ExplorationService.fetchExamples uses 'headword' as the parameter name but every HTTP handler and query parameter uses 'seed', creating a vocabulary mismatch at the service boundary
evidence: ExplorationService.fetchExamples signature: fetchExamples(@NonNull String collocate, @NonNull String headword, @NonNull RelationConfig relationConfig, @NonNull FetchExamplesOptions opts)
handleSemanticFieldExamples in ExplorationHandlers: String seed = requireParam(params, "seed"); ... semanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples(collocate, seed, resolvedConfig, ...)
All other exploration methods use 'seed' / 'seeds' consistently in both interface and HTTP layer
issues.suggestion: Rename the 'headword' parameter in ExplorationService.fetchExamples (and its implementation in SemanticFieldExplorer) to 'seed' to align with the vocabulary used everywhere else in the exploration API.

issues.summary: The two concordance-examples endpoints (/api/semantic-field/examples and /api/concordance/examples) accept identical parameters but return different response fields, making them harder to use interchangeably
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExamples response includes 'total_results' field but no 'fallback' field
handleConcordanceExamples response includes a 'fallback: true' field (when fallback logic fires) but no 'total_results' field
Both endpoints accept the same query parameters: seed, collocate, relation, top — the differing response shapes are not signalled by any API documentation at the HTTP layer
issues.suggestion: Align the two endpoints' response shapes: add 'total_results' to the concordance examples response and define whether 'fallback' belongs in both. Extract a shared response builder (similar to how ExploreResponseAssembler.collocateToExampleMap is already shared) to enforce the common shape.

root_causes: Response assembly evolved organically: explore and compare were implemented at different times and the naming conventions diverged without a shared vocabulary enforced at the model layer
The 'adjectives' key is a historical artifact from when the comparison feature was conceived specifically for adjective profiles, before the system became grammatical-relation-agnostic
likely_symptoms: compare_adjectives_key_mismatch is a direct symptom of the domain-vocabulary leaking into what should be a relation-agnostic API
